city_living_costs: Kiel is relatively affordable with monthly living costs around €800-1,000 including rent. Student cafeterias offer meals for €3-5, and the compact city center reduces transportation costs.
german_daily_life_requirement: B1 German minimum recommended for comfortable daily life in Kiel. While the university area is somewhat international, shops, services, and social activities primarily operate in German.
english_livability: English-only living is challenging in Kiel outside university settings. Most locals speak some English but German is essential for housing matters, bureaucracy, and integrating into local life beyond campus.

tab_overview:
Course location
Trier
Teaching language
German
Language level of course
German: A1, A2, B1, B2, C1
Date(s)
3 August - 28 August 2026 (Registration deadline of course provider: 15 July 2026)
Target group
Students and graduates of all subjects
Description/content
1. Language courses
The language courses cover elementary, intermediate, and advanced levels (A1, A2, B1, B2, C1). There are a total of 75 hours (at 45 min.) of lessons. The main goal is to expand practical language skills in the areas of reading and listening comprehension as well as speaking and writing. The courses also focus on improving grammatical, lexical, and idiomatic skills. Authentic real-world texts are used from literature, science, and�media, including TV and radio news. This course helps students learn German so that they can study in Germany.
All students will take a placement test on the first day of class. The course concludes with a final test, after which a certificate (worth five ECTS points) will be awarded.
2. Didactics and methodology of teaching and learning German as a foreign language (DaF)
Special classes are offered for teachers of German and students of German studies with an interest in intercultural subjects. In these classes, current didactic methods and pedagogical concepts in the field of German as a foreign language will be discussed. Among other topics, the focus will be on media didactics, e-learning, intercultural didactics, and new teaching materials (approx. 28 hours).
If participants additionally attend the language course for advanced learners of German (C1) as well as the academic seminars and lectures, they will be awarded a certificate ("Zertifikat Didaktik und Methodik Deutsch als Fremdsprache") upon successful completion of the course.
3. Academic lecture and seminar programme
An interdisciplinary lecture and seminar programme is provided by the Departments of Language and Literature, Politics and History, Social Science and Economics (approx. 12 hours in total, 45 minutes each).
(For students at an elementary level (A1, A2, B1), conversation classes are offered instead.)
4. Cultural programme
Cultural events and excursions to the Moselle region, Luxembourg, and France complement the language courses and lectures.

Upon request, accommodation can be provided in one of the university's student accommodation halls.

Meals
Not included, but available in the university canteens/cafeterias and in the shops nearby
Is a social and leisure programme offered?
Yes
Description of social and leisure programme
Our leisure programme:
Three full-day excursions
to the
Moselle
(distance from Trier: 50 km), to
Luxembourg
(distance approx. 45 km) and
France
(e.g. Metz or Nancy)
Guided tours
to the sights of Trier (UNESCO World Heritage Site, buildings of the Roman Emperor Constantine), museum visits, city tours
Walking tours
/ hikes (e.g. to Cochem Castle on the Moselle), concert visits (e.g. organ music in the Roman Palastaula "Constantine Basilica"), etc.
In addition, the tutors of the International Summer Course (IFK) offer
numerous activities in the evenings
(sports, pub crawl, barbecue, dance class, beach volleyball, karaoke, game nights, international dinner and many more).
The excursions, the guided tours and the visit of the organ concert are already included in the course fee. For individual further offers, small additional costs may be incurred under certain circumstances. This will be communicated in time during the course.
All participants are supervised by our IFK tutors.

city_expats_overview: Trier is Germany's oldest city with UNESCO World Heritage sites and a safe, walkable historic center perfect for students. The small-city atmosphere (110,000 residents) offers authentic German immersion with a growing international student community.
accommodation_outlook: University accommodation is available for €490 for the entire 4-week program in student halls. This is excellent value and highly recommended as private short-term rentals are harder to find and more expensive (€600-800/month).
job_market_demand: As a short summer course, job opportunities aren't the focus, but Trier's proximity to Luxembourg (45km) offers excellent prospects for German speakers in finance and EU institutions. The wine and tourism industries also value multilingual professionals.
city_living_costs: Budget €800-1000 total for the month including accommodation (€490), meals in university cafeterias (€150-200), and personal expenses. Trier is one of Germany's more affordable cities.
german_daily_life_requirement: A2-B1 German helps significantly for daily life in Trier as it's less international than major cities. However, the summer school provides support and many locals speak some English, especially near the university.
english_livability: The summer school provides English support for beginners, but outside campus, German is helpful for shopping, restaurants, and socializing. The program's tutors assist with daily life challenges, making English-only manageable during the course.
university_profile: Trier University, founded in 1473 and re-established in 1970, excels in humanities, social sciences, and economics with strong international partnerships. The International Summer School has run for over 50 years, demonstrating exceptional expertise in German language education and cultural integration for international students.
how_to_apply: Apply directly through http://www.iss.uni-trier.de by July 15, 2026. The university provides visa support for international students.

Information on dates, prices and mode of study
Language courses, free time activities in Wittenberg and two day trips are included in the course fee.
Target group
This course is open for everyone interested in the German language.
Description/content
The goal of the Summer Academy is to impart knowledge combining both the culture of Germany and the German language. Through selected regional topics, the many facets of modern German life will be discovered in activities such as conversations with experts, presentations, excursions, and a final course project. With participants from around the world, knowledge exchange and intercultural learning will be significant aspects of this summer course. All courses have lessons on pronunciation.
After a full day of learning and gathering new impressions, a variety of free time activities and intercultural get-togethers will help you to enjoy this wonderful summer course.

city_expats_overview: Lutherstadt Wittenberg is a compact Elbe River town best known for its Reformation heritage, drawing international students to Martin Luther University’s summer programs amid UNESCO-listed streets. Its manageable size and strong cultural programming make it easy for newcomers to integrate quickly while enjoying frequent excursions to nearby historical sites.
accommodation_outlook: Short-term participants typically stay in university-arranged dorm rooms or vetted host families, with monthly costs ranging from €450-650 including utilities; private rentals are scarce, so early coordination with the program office is essential. Long-term leases in the wider town average €8-10/m², but availability is limited compared with larger cities.
job_market_demand: The local job market is small and centered on tourism, education, and municipal services, offering only occasional part-time roles for German-speaking students. Those seeking internships in education or cultural management usually commute to Halle or Leipzig, where Martin Luther University maintains broader networks.
city_living_costs: Expect €650-900 per month: €450-650 for housing, €120-180 for food, €30 for local transport or bike upkeep, and €50-80 for leisure and study materials. Costs stay well below big-city levels thanks to short distances and limited nightlife spending.
german_daily_life_requirement: A2 German is advisable for errands, shopping, and interacting with local services, as many residents prefer German in daily exchanges. Program staff can assist, but basic conversational ability greatly eases integration.
english_livability: English is understood by university staff and younger residents involved with the summer academy, yet outside academic settings English proficiency drops, so students should not rely on it for everyday tasks. Cultural venues often provide bilingual materials, but administrative offices expect German communication.
university_profile: Martin Luther University Halle-Wittenberg is one of Germany's oldest universities (founded 1502), renowned for humanities and German studies. The Institute for German Language and Culture specializes in immersive language education with strong cultural programming and personalized support for international learners.
how_to_apply: Submit application directly to Institut für deutsche Sprache und Kultur, Collegienstraße 62, 06886 Lutherstadt Wittenberg. Deadlines vary by session (April 30 - July 17, 2026).

In addition to language instruction for scientific purposes, lectures are offered in German social studies, literature, and geography ("Landeskunde") (minimum number of participants: 15).
We offer additional preparation courses for TestDaF.
The courses include 24 classroom hours (45 minutes per classroom hour) per week on levels B1-C1. The programme prepares participants for language and study requirements at German universities. Participants will also obtain an invitation letter in order to apply for a visa.

Description/content
The a.r.t.e.s. Graduate School for the Humanities Cologne is the graduate school of the University of Colognes Faculty of Arts and Humanities. The Faculty of Arts and Humanities  one of the largest of its kind in Europe  creates ideal conditions for an intensive, lively, and open-minded exchange of ideas and offers a unique range of subjects in the humanities and cultural studies, including, for example, philosophy, language and literature studies, linguistics, history and art history, ethnology and regional studies, media and theatre studies, musicology, archaeology, and digital humanities.
Since October 2012, the a.r.t.e.s Graduate School is responsible for every doctoral project at the Faculty of Arts and Humanities. Doctoral students can choose between a regular track and an integrated track. The regular track is the standard model of the individual doctorate, whereas the integrated track is the structured doctoral model that, with regard to the interdisciplinary scope, is designed to allow completion of the doctoral project within a time frame of three years. The integrated track also includes a funding option for the pre-doc phase (six months) and a full scholarship for three years.
The a.r.t.e.s. Graduate School serves as a platform for the internationalisation of graduate programmes that can build on a formidable existing network of cooperation agreements in the faculty and draw from the experience of a great number of teaching and research centres. The concept of the a.r.t.e.s. Graduate School is completed by a special focus on equality standards.
The a.r.t.e.s. integrated track addresses graduates who have completed their studies with outstanding academic results in the humanities and who are interested in beginning their doctoral studies shortly after their final examination. The participants are supervised by two graduate class mentors and three academic supervisors, one of whom is from another discipline. In the graduate class colloquia during the semester period, the doctoral candidates periodically discuss the progress of their research with their classmates and the class mentors. The yearly and comprehensive colloquia of the entire graduate school offer the opportunity to discuss topics and advances in research.
tab_course_details:
Course organisation
The integrated track of the a.r.t.e.s. Graduate School provides a structured doctoral model with an interdisciplinary approach. The individual doctoral studies are combined with a set of guided courses. Supervision is provided through a mentoring programme during a period of three academic years. The first year of the integrated track focuses on the elaboration of the dissertation topic, which has to be presented in the a.r.t.e.s. colloquium. In the second year, students focus on their own research and can attend additional workshops on transferable skills (e.g. seminars in academic presentation and writing, third party-funding, etc.), while the third year is dedicated to the presentation of results, writing, and the completion of the doctoral dissertation.
The a.r.t.e.s. collegiate programme offers the opportunity to take part in the integrated track programme without a scholarship from the Graduate School, including the opportunity to attend the graduate classes.
A Diploma supplement will be issued
No
International elements
International guest lecturers
Language training provided
Projects with partners in Germany and abroad
Content-related regional focus
Specialist literature in other languages
Description of other international elements
The a.r.t.e.s. Graduate School emphasises research, teaching, and networking on an international level. This is apparent not only in the many transcultural and regionally-diverse research projects pursued at the school. It is also evident in the bilateral research and international doctoral models allowed for at a.r.t.e.s. Two examples of these are the binational joint supervision (cotutelle de th�se) and the supplementary qualification Doctor Europaeus, both of which contribute to the internationalisation of academic research.
The refinement of the international profile of a.r.t.e.s. and that of the entire Faculty of Arts and Humanities is contributed to by its Summer Schools and Graduate Seminars, which are hosted regularly. It cooperates closely with its partner institutions and continually expands its international network.
Teaching/work obligations or opportunities
The programme does not include additional teaching or work obligations.

None
Semester contribution
The semester contribution amounts to approx. 320 EUR per semester. The fee includes a "Deutschlandsemesterticket" that covers public transport in Germany.
Funding opportunities within the university
Yes
Description of the above-mentioned funding opportunities within the university
Each year, the a.r.t.e.s. Graduate School funds up to eight full scholarships for the integrated track amounting to 1,600 EUR per month for a maximum of six semesters. Students also receive financial aid for travels that benefit the research project.
In addition and to ensure a quick transition from Masters or similar degrees to doctoral studies, the a.r.t.e.s. Graduate School offers each year up to eight Predoc scholarships for six months (1,200 EUR per month) to help candidates to elaborate a doctoral project.

Check for current openings on our website
www.asp.uni-jena.de/open_positions
and apply within the stated deadlines. We also accept unsolicited applications at any time.
Tuition fees per semester in EUR
None
Combined Master's degree / PhD programme
Yes
Joint degree / double degree programme
No
Description/content
Photonics is one of the main research fields at the Friedrich Schiller University Jena, and about 300 doctoral candidates are currently enrolled in our research-oriented PhD/doctoral programme.
In our optics /�photonics and quantum technology programme, we offer:
high-end research and education in a world-class scientific environment with intensive mentoring
PhD topics at the frontiers of science, giving way to the opportunity to publish in the world's most renowned journals like Nature and Science
some of the best-equipped optics and photonics labs in the world
an international programme with modules focusing on scientific and technical skills and equipping you with transferable skills
access to an elite network of experts in optics and photonics
One important cornerstone of our philosophy is to regard and value our doctoral candidates as scientists in all respects. The ASP offers an individually adjusted programme to meet each candidate's scientific background and interests. This guarantees an optimal level of preparation for a high-profile career in research or industry.
tab_course_details:
Course organisation
In addition to the regular courses which are part of the ASP programme, a variety of special lectures and workshops is given by
world-renowned guest professors
. Our open photonics makerspace at the
Lichtwerkstatt Jena
is always open for young people with creativity and some ideas. Moreover, doctoral candidates benefit from the close collaboration between ASP and some of the world's leading companies in the field of optics and photonics, such as ZEISS, ASML, TRUMPF, SCHOTT or JENOPTIK, to name just a few.
Interdisciplinarity is an inherent part of our philosophy. ASP provides a variety of courses that help students acquire additional knowledge in complementary sciences as well as essential transferable skills, e.g., in project management, leadership and developing a scientific career. Furthermore, workshops and courses on the German language, intercultural competences, job application training and good scientific practice are specifically designed to accommodate our international students quickly to their new scientific and private environment in Jena.
A Diploma supplement will be issued
Yes
International elements
International guest lecturers
Language training provided
Projects with partners in Germany and abroad
Training in intercultural skills
Courses are led with foreign partners
Content-related regional focus
International comparisons and thematic reference to the international context
Integrated internships
We are permanently offering the possibility of individual internships with our regional industry partners (e.g., ZEISS, ASML, TRUMPF, SCHOTT, JENOPTIK) as well as research stays abroad with our large network of international partners.
Teaching/work obligations or opportunities
Beside top-notch research, teaching undergraduate students is part of the professional experience that each of our doctoral students will gain.
Special promotion / funding of the programme
ERASMUS+
DFG (e.g. Research Training Groups)
Other (e.g. state level)
Helmholtz Graduate Schools

Yes
Course-specific, integrated English language courses
Yes
tab_costs_funding:
Tuition fees per semester in EUR
None
Semester contribution
Doctoral candidates in Jena have the possibility to enrol at the Friedrich Schiller University. Enrolment is not compulsory but it offers a whole range of advantages like access to student dorms, a "mensa" (student dining hall) discount, free public transport, a campus card, and more. The semester fee is approximately 250 EUR.
https://www.uni-jena.de/en/semesterbeitrag
Costs of living
The living costs in Jena are still low compared to other places in Germany or elsewhere. We recommend that you plan for a budget of at least 700 to 770 EUR per month for living expenses. However, if you are accepted to our doctoral programme, you will be contracted and earn an even higher salary. In general, the costs of living are usually not an issue for any of our PhD/doctoral students.
https://www.uni-jena.de/en/jena-living-international
Funding opportunities within the university
Yes
Description of the above-mentioned funding opportunities within the university
Once accepted into our programme, our specialised administration will intensively assist you in finding the right funding opportunity.
tab_requirements_registration:
Academic admission requirements
Applicants must have obtained or be about to obtain a Master's degree or equivalent qualification in physics, optics, engineering, or a related field.
Language requirements
We request a decent level of English language capabilities, as English is common in the international science community. Test certificates like Cambridge, IELTS or TOEFL are appreciated.
Knowledge of the German language is NOT a necessary prerequisite to be enrolled in our programme.
Application deadline
Check for current openings on our website
www.asp.uni-jena.de/open_positions
and apply within the stated deadlines. We also accept unsolicited applications at any time.
Submit application to
Applications can be submitted at any time at:
https://apply2.uni-jena.de/utumg
.

Once accepted by our programme, support in finding accommodation is available through our local ASP office. For a quick start, the rent for a single room in a university guest house is�between 300 and 500 EUR.
Structured research and supervision
Yes
Research training / discussion

Career advisory services and programmes for future professionals
ASP offers intensive support to any individual PhD/doctoral candidate, including job application trainings, academic career counselling, start-up support, and access to a vast�network of industry partners and exclusive academic institutions all over the world.

General services and support for international students and doctoral candidates
ASP strongly and actively pursues gender mainstreaming and family-friendly working conditions. In particular, the competitive support instruments that aim to maximally link doctoral student and postdoctoral career stages are considered to be optimally designed for promoting gender equality and for balancing disadvantages for female candidates. The support of young female scientists is a cross-sectional task for both the ASP and the Friedrich Schiller University, and it is taken seriously by both. It creates and secures conditions of equal opportunity for all its members, regardless of gender and background.

accommodation_outlook: University guest houses offer single rooms for 300-500 EUR upon acceptance. The ASP office provides dedicated accommodation support, and the smaller city size makes housing more accessible than in larger German cities.
job_market_demand: Exceptional job prospects with world-leading optics companies headquartered locally (ZEISS, JENOPTIK, SCHOTT). The region is Germany's photonics hub with strong demand for PhD graduates in both industry and academia.
city_living_costs: Monthly budget of 700-770 EUR covers all living expenses including rent. Jena remains one of Germany's most affordable university cities, with PhD salaries easily covering costs.
german_daily_life_requirement: A2-B1 German helpful for daily life outside campus, but the international research community and student services make A1 sufficient initially. The program offers integrated German language courses.
english_livability: Full English instruction and supervision available for the PhD program. Campus services and research environment fully English-compatible, though basic German helps for city administration and social integration.
university_profile: Friedrich Schiller University Jena is Germany's premier photonics research hub, hosting 300+ doctoral candidates in optics. The university excels in physics and engineering, with world-class facilities including the Lichtwerkstatt Jena makerspace and partnerships with global tech leaders.

city_expats_overview: Munich is Germany's third-largest city with a vibrant international community and excellent safety record. The city hosts numerous cultural events and has strong English-speaking expat networks, though living costs are among Germany's highest.
accommodation_outlook: Munich has Germany's tightest housing market with rooms typically €600-900/month. SDI offers on-campus rooms which is advantageous; otherwise start searching WG-Gesucht and Studenten-WG months in advance.
job_market_demand: Munich offers excellent job prospects with major employers like BMW, Siemens, and Allianz, plus a thriving startup scene. Student jobs are competitive but available in hospitality, tutoring, and retail sectors.
city_living_costs: Expect €1,000-1,500 monthly total expenses in Munich (rent €600-900, food €200-300, transport €60, miscellaneous €150-250). Budget carefully as Munich is 30-40% more expensive than other German cities.
german_daily_life_requirement: B1-B2 German strongly recommended for daily life in Munich. While central areas have English speakers, German is essential for housing searches, bureaucracy, and local integration.
english_livability: English-only living is challenging in Munich outside tourist areas and international companies. While many speak English, German is needed for administrative tasks, finding housing, and social integration beyond expat circles.
university_profile: SDI München specializes in languages, communication, and international business with a focus on practical, career-oriented education. The university maintains small class sizes and strong industry connections, particularly in translation, interpreting, and international management fields.
how_to_apply: Submit application directly to SDI München at Baierbrunner Straße 28, 81379 München, Germany. No specific deadline mentioned, apply early for visa processing time.

city_expats_overview: Lutherstadt Wittenberg is a compact Elbe-side town best known for its Reformation heritage, drawing international students to Martin Luther University’s satellite facilities and cultural institutes. Daily life revolves around the historic Altstadt, where most services, cafés, and student events are within walking distance of the Schloss- and Stadtkirche landmarks.
accommodation_outlook: Short-term program participants typically use university-arranged dorm rooms or vetted host families, with monthly costs around €420–€520 for a furnished single in student housing and €550–€650 for half-board homestays. Private rentals are scarce and often require commuting from Dessau or Halle, so early coordination with the academy is essential.
job_market_demand: The local job market is limited to tourism, museum services, and small educational institutions; short-term students usually rely on stipends rather than local employment. Occasional part-time roles exist in visitor services or language tutoring, but German proficiency and prior permits are mandatory.
city_living_costs: Expect €850–€1,000 per month including €450 rent, €200 for groceries and dining, €80 for transport or bike upkeep, and €120–€200 for leisure, insurance, and incidentals. Prices remain lower than in Leipzig or Berlin, but specialty goods may require trips to Halle.
german_daily_life_requirement: A2 German is advisable for errands, medical appointments, and municipal offices, as most staff default to German and signage is rarely bilingual. University staff can assist in English, yet everyday interactions—bakeries, pharmacies, train counters—assume basic German competence.
english_livability: English is understood within program offices and major museums, but outside tourist hotspots many residents prefer German, making English-only living inconvenient. Students relying solely on English should expect slower service and occasional misunderstandings in shops or public services.
university_profile: Martin Luther University Halle-Wittenberg is one of Germany's oldest universities (founded 1502/1694), renowned for humanities, theology, and German studies. The Institute for German Language and Culture specializes in intensive language programs with strong cultural integration and personalized support for international learners.
how_to_apply: Submit application directly to Martin-Luther-Universität Halle-Wittenberg, Institut für deutsche Sprache und Kultur, Collegienstraße 62, 06886 Lutherstadt Wittenberg by January 2, 2026.

Pace of course

Information on dates, prices and mode of study
Our courses in Leipzig combine face-to-face learning with modern digital methods. In addition to varied on-site activities, we supplement the lessons with an intuitive learning platform that serves as a central location for materials and exchange.
If necessary, learning can take place entirely online: You can take part in live video conferences and deepen your knowledge during independent learning phases.
Our learning service is there to support you throughout the course.
Target group
Our fall course is aimed at international researchers, doctoral candidates, and students who are about to start their academic work or studies in Germany and would like to improve their German language skills in a targeted and practical way.
Description/content
Language skills for your studies  discover and experience Leipzig
The Academic Life in Leipzig autumn course combines intensive German lessons with exciting insights into Leipzig as a centre of science and higher education. This course is ideal for�anyone looking to improve their language skills while gaining first-hand experience of academic life in Germany.
Learn German  intensively and individually
Online placement test before the start of the course and personal oral assessment at the start
Learn in small groups (approx. 14 participants)  from level A1 to C1
Approx. 25 lessons per week, including phonetics training in individual or small group sessions
Modern textbook (included in the course fee), up-to-date materials, and access to the Moodle learning platform
Modules on academic speaking and writing
Practical explorations on the topic of "Studying and researching in Leipzig"  in cooperation with Leipzig universities and research institutions
Written and oral final exam
Acquisition of five ECTS points possible (can be credited at the home university)
Certificate of participation with documented test results
Experience Germany  culture and everyday life up close
Interactive cultural events with a focus on real-life regional studies  e.g. research and interviews on site
City walks and exploratory tours in and around Leipzig
Visits to research institutions in Leipzig
One full-day excursion
Personal support from the course team
Further optional offers (e.g. weekend excursions to Dresden or Weimar)

tab_services:
Is accommodation organised?
The organiser helps participants look for accommodation.
Meals
Our course participants can eat at the student cafeteria "Mensa am Park", which is part of the campus of Leipzig University in the city centre.
Close to our institute as well as in the nearby city centre, you will also find a wide variety of caf�s, restaurants, and pubs.
Moreover, there are small kitchens in the accommodation, where you can prepare meals on your own.
Is a social and leisure programme offered?
Yes
Description of social and leisure programme
The course price includes a varied accompanying programme that combines learning with shared experiences:
one full-day excursion, e.g. to Erfurt  exciting impressions and new perspectives outside of the seminar room
shared opening and closing dinners  a great way to arrive and bring the course to a close together
selected cultural events, e.g. a concert  for special evenings full of music and encounters
visits to museums and places of interest, e.g. a guided tour of the Forum of Contemporary History  bringing history to life
We also offer other interesting excursions, such as an excursion to Dresden. This is bookable for a small surcharge.
We are happy to give personal tips for your own activities so that you can discover Leipzig and the surrounding area in your own way.

Yes

city_expats_overview: Leipzig combines a historic university tradition with a lively arts scene centered around the Südvorstadt and Plagwitz districts, making it attractive to international researchers and students. The city’s compact layout and strong cycling culture help newcomers integrate quickly into academic and social life.
accommodation_outlook: Shared flats (WGs) in Leipzig typically cost €320-450 per month, while small studios range €500-650, with highest demand near the city center and Schleußig. University housing services and the Studentenwerk offer limited dorm rooms, so early applications are essential, especially for autumn arrivals.
job_market_demand: Leipzig’s job market favors research assistants in the life sciences, digital humanities, and environmental studies thanks to institutions like the Helmholtz and Max Planck centers, while the city’s growing media and logistics sectors offer part-time roles for German-speaking students. English-only positions exist mainly in international research projects but remain limited.
city_living_costs: Monthly expenses average €850-1,050, including €350-500 for rent, €220 for food, €65 for the LVB semester transport ticket, and €200-250 for insurance, study materials, and leisure. Energy surcharges have risen slightly since 2024, so budgeting a small buffer is prudent.
german_daily_life_requirement: A2 German suffices for groceries and cafés, but B1 is recommended for dealing with the Bürgeramt, health insurance, and landlord communication, as many offices still operate primarily in German. Locals are generally patient with learners, yet official paperwork rarely offers English translations.
english_livability: Leipzig University departments and research institutes often conduct seminars in English, and many younger residents speak it confidently, especially in academic settings. Outside campus, English support is patchier, so relying solely on English can slow down bureaucratic processes and housing searches.
university_profile: Leipzig University, founded in 1409, is one of Germany's oldest universities with strong programs in humanities, sciences, and medicine. The university ranks well nationally and offers excellent support for international students through programs like interDaF, with a particularly strong reputation in German studies and cultural research.
how_to_apply: Send the registration form to interDaF by mail (Lumumbastraße 4, 04105 Leipzig) or email (interdaf@uni-leipzig.de). Contact them directly for pricing and specific deadlines.

Description/content
Starting your studies abroad means that changes lie ahead  whether it's the new language, making new friends or achieving success in your academic courses. The ACCESS� programme offers a comprehensive preparation package for prospective engineering or business students.
Programme components include:
an intensive German language course that includes the levels B1 and B2
specialised subjects in engineering (e.g. programming, maths, electrical engineering), business (e.g. marketing, accounting, economics) or informatics in the English language
preparation for academic working methods (e.g. presentation skills, academic writing)
excursions to companies in the region
Additional support:
intercultural buddy programme
contact to student assistants regarding questions about campus life
weekly consultation hours

Also, the programme provides an introduction to the following Bachelor's programmes:
Engineering Profile:
Electrical Systems Engineering (BEng)
Automotive Systems Engineering (BEng)
Mechatronics and Robotics (BEng)
Mechanical Engineering
Business Profile:

Successful participants can apply directly for the Bachelor's programmes of their choice. Course achievements from the preparation programme can be recognised in the above-mentioned Bachelor's programmes upon request.

city_expats_overview: Heilbronn sits on the Neckar River in northern Baden-Württemberg and combines a compact city center with a sizable international community drawn by its universities and industry. International students appreciate the manageable scale, reliable public transport, and quick rail links to Stuttgart, Mannheim, and Würzburg.
accommodation_outlook: Student dorm rooms via Studierendenwerk Heilbronn-Franken start around 280–350 EUR, while WG rooms in districts like Sontheim or Böckingen typically cost 350–450 EUR; private studios often exceed 500 EUR and require early applications due to limited supply. Many students broaden their search to nearby towns such as Neckarsulm or Weinsberg for better availability.
job_market_demand: Heilbronn’s economy is anchored by automotive suppliers, logistics, and food processing, with companies like Audi (Neckarsulm), Bosch, and Lidl’s Schwarz Group offering internships and entry roles for engineering, IT, and business graduates. The city’s Innovation Park AI and logistics hub also create demand for data, automation, and supply-chain talent.
city_living_costs: A realistic monthly budget is 800–950 EUR: rent 320–450 EUR, food 220–260 EUR, health insurance 120 EUR, transport 49 EUR Deutschlandticket, and 80–120 EUR for leisure and incidentals. Costs remain below Stuttgart but slightly above smaller rural towns.
german_daily_life_requirement: B1 German is advisable for dealing with landlords, city offices, and everyday errands, though basic A2 can suffice initially if students actively practice and use university support services.
english_livability: English works well on campus and within international programs, yet outside the university many service staff and municipal offices expect German, so students should be prepared to switch languages.
university_profile: Heilbronn University of Applied Sciences specializes in practical, industry-oriented education with strong programs in engineering, business, and informatics. Known for close industry partnerships and hands-on learning approach, the university ranks well among German universities of applied sciences for graduate employability.
how_to_apply: Submit application directly through the university website at https://www.hs-heilbronn.de/en/access2. Program starts in September each year.

tab_services:
Possibility of finding part-time employment
Student assistant jobs are available on a competitive basis at the university & its departments, at the affiliated research institutes of the Science & Innovation Alliance Kaiserslautern (including DFKI, Max Planck, Fraunhofer, etc.), or at nearby companies (John Deere, Daimler, BASF, etc.).
Excellent students have good chances of finding jobs in research projects, but these are not usually granted in advance.
International Affairs RPTU supports international students in finding student jobs and in strengthening their professional network, e.g. by organising networking dialogues with�executives from science and industry.
Accommodation
RPTU Kaiserslautern
The majority of our students live in residence halls located on or close to campus. The "Studierendenwerk" operates several large halls of residence in Kaiserslautern. Students submit housing applications directly to the Studierendenwerk. For international Master's or PhD students, International Affairs RPTU aims to arrange accommodation in these residence halls or in private accommodation as part of the pre-arrival service. The halls of residence have modern single, double or shared apartments with kitchenette, bathroom/WC, and modern facilities as well as internet access.
Fully-furnished: kitchenette (no plates & pots, etc.), bed & mattress (no blankets & pillows), wardrobe, shelf, table, chair (no computer)  rent: approx. 250 to 350 EUR
Since accommodation is not automatically guaranteed with admission, a request for accommodation to ISGS is advisable.
Optional: To help international students get started in Kaiserslautern, the Studierendenwerk offers a starter package. This package can be purchased for 95 EUR:
https://www.studierendenwerk-kaiserslautern.de/en/housing/international
.
More information:
https://rptu.de/international/master/prospective-students/travel-accommodation/accommodation
RPTU Landau
Accommodation is available in student residences or on the private housing market. A certain number of rooms in student residences are reserved for incoming international Master's students who enrol at RPTU Landau. Students can contact the Welcome Center for further information with regard to the application process or a search for private housing. E-mail:
international@uni-landau.de
More information:
https://rptu.de/international/master/prospective-students-landau/travel-accommodation/accommodation
Career advisory services and programmes for future professionals
International Affairs RPTU sets up interdisciplinary workshops, seminars and info sessions focusing on skills & competences needed to establish a career in the German (business) culture.
More information:
https://rptu.de/international/master/current-students/enhance-your-degree

General services and support for international students and doctoral candidates
International Affairs RPTU supports international students during every phase of their stay!
Before your arrival: Effective pre-arrival communication helps you handle application, visa, residence permit, and housing affairs.
Once you are here: As a warm-up to your studies, we offer an orientation course which combines German language training with administrative support (finding accommodation, registration process, visa, health insurance, rental contracts, etc.) & a cultural & social component including individual guidance in the first days, a campus & city tour, excursions, info sessions, social events, a welcome party, etc.
During your stay: We provide you with continuing support and accompany you on your way until graduation and entry into the German job market.
More information:
https://rptu.de/en/international/home

city_expats_overview: Kaiserslautern is a compact Palatinate city with a large student share and a notable international community thanks to RPTU and the nearby U.S. military presence. International students find a manageable city center, easy cycling distances, and supportive university services tailored to newcomers.
accommodation_outlook: Student housing is competitive but manageable: furnished dorm rooms via Studierendenwerk typically cost 280-360 EUR, while private shared flats range 350-450 EUR per room depending on proximity to campus. Early applications and temporary sublets are common strategies during peak semesters.
job_market_demand: Actuarial and financial mathematics students tap into regional employers such as insurance analytics teams, financial IT firms, and research institutes like DFKI and Fraunhofer IESE, plus nearby corporate hubs in Mannheim and Frankfurt reachable by train. On-campus research assistantships and collaborations with local fintech startups provide practical experience.
city_living_costs: Average monthly expenses run about 900 EUR: 320 EUR rent, 120 EUR health insurance, 220 EUR food, 80 EUR transport/semester fee, and 160 EUR for study materials and leisure. Costs stay below big-city levels but require budgeting for occasional trips to larger hubs.
german_daily_life_requirement: A2 German suffices for groceries, bureaucracy, and casual interactions, while B1 smooths dealings with landlords and municipal offices; many locals appreciate attempts to speak German even when switching to English.
english_livability: University administration, lectures, and research groups in the program operate comfortably in English, and many residents have basic English due to the U.S. base, yet off-campus errands still benefit from some German proficiency.
university_profile: RPTU Kaiserslautern-Landau excels in applied mathematics and technology, with strong industry partnerships through the Science & Innovation Alliance. The university is particularly renowned for its mathematics department and offers excellent support for international students through comprehensive pre-arrival and ongoing assistance programs.
how_to_apply: Submit applications directly to the Graduate School 'Mathematics as a Key Technology' at RPTU Kaiserslautern-Landau by April 30 (winter) or October 31 (summer). Details at https://math.rptu.de/en/studies/study-programmes/msc/application-master

Target group
International students who only have very little or no knowledge of the German language and who are aiming for a degree programme at the University of Passau
Description/content
This intensive German course is a joint project between the Language Centre and the International Office of the University of Passau, and it is one of the building blocks of the universitys strong international and multicultural orientation.
The Academic German Year (AGY) starts with level A1 of the Common European Framework of Reference for Languages. Those completing the course will be given ample preparation to pass an official German language proficiency test.
Study German together with highly motivated international students! The course participants will take intensive German classes five days a week (24 units each 45 minutes) and will be taught by experienced lecturers.
Applicants should provide the following documents:
A cover letter (letter of motivation) in German or English, one page
Curriculum vitae in German or English
A photocopy or scan of your
secondary school leaving certificate
Examples of leaving certificates are as follows: A Levels, Apolytirio, Baccalaur�at, Gaokao, high school diploma, Lise Diplomas1, Matura, Maturita, Scottish Highers, Te'udat bagrut, T�tulo de Bachillerato + Pruebas de Aptitud. (This list is not exhaustive.)
Please also include certificates for all academic achievements gained after secondary school, such as university transcripts and certificates.
Applicants need to fill out an application form:
https://www.gcp.uni-passau.de/en/academic-german-year/applications-and-admissions

Is accommodation organised?
The organiser helps participants look for accommodation.
Meals
In the award-winning refectory in Passau, each student can put together his or her own menu, choosing from four different dishes every day during the lecture period. These meals include vegetarian dishes, meat dishes, soups, salads and desserts. Payment is done electronically with your "CampusCard".
Thanks to state subsidies, prices are kept low.
Is a social and leisure programme offered?
Yes
Description of social and leisure programme
The extracurricular activities offer you many opportunities to get to know Germany's culture, landscape and people.
In addition to the German courses, we offer exciting trips  for example, to the Bavarian Forest National Park�and to Munich, Regensburg�and Linz. Additionally, we organise parties, picnics, and barbecues on the popular "Innwiese" directly by the river, and we also offer seminars and much more.

city_expats_overview: Passau is a charming Bavarian university town at the confluence of three rivers, offering a safe and welcoming environment for international students. The city has a growing international community thanks to the university's 12,000+ students from over 100 countries.
accommodation_outlook: Housing assistance is provided by the organizers, with student dorms typically costing €250-400/month and shared flats (WG) ranging €350-500. Start searching early as the small city size means limited options, especially before semester start.
job_market_demand: Limited part-time opportunities for non-German speakers during the language course, but Passau's proximity to Austria and Czech Republic opens cross-border opportunities once German improves. Tourism and university-related jobs are most accessible.
city_living_costs: Budget €700-900 monthly total: rent (€300-450), food (€200-250), health insurance (€110), and personal expenses (€100-150). The subsidized university cafeteria and included bus pass with CampusCard help reduce costs.
german_daily_life_requirement: A2-B1 German needed for comfortable daily life in this smaller Bavarian city. While the university has international services, shops, bureaucracy, and social life outside campus require basic German.
english_livability: English-only living is challenging in Passau outside the university bubble. While fellow international students and university staff speak English, German is essential for housing searches, shopping, and integrating into local life.
university_profile: University of Passau is known for its strong international orientation with partnerships in 240+ universities worldwide, excellent language programs, and strengths in computer science, business, and European studies. The compact campus and 1:18 student-teacher ratio create a supportive learning environment, particularly valued for humanities and social sciences.
how_to_apply: Submit application directly to University of Passau via online form at https://www.gcp.uni-passau.de/en/academic-german-year/applications-and-admissions by June 30, 2026.
